title: End of module: Eigenanalysis of Covariance Matrices

questions.text: Stacking a WSS process into a lag vector $\mathbf{x}(n) = [x(n), x(n-1)]^T$, the entries of its covariance matrix $\Sigma = E[\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}(n)^T]$ are:
choices: Autocorrelation values: diagonal entries R_x(0), off-diagonal entries R_x(1)
Always exactly the identity matrix regardless of the process
The process's mean value repeated in every entry
questions.explanation: The diagonal entries are E[x(n)x(n)] and E[x(n-1)x(n-1)], both equal to R_x(0); the off-diagonal entries are E[x(n)x(n-1)] = R_x(1). Any WSS process's lag-vector covariance matrix is built directly from its autocorrelation function.

questions.text: A covariance matrix's eigenvectors and eigenvalues represent:
choices: The directions the data varies along, and the variances along those directions
The mean value of the data along each axis
The sample rate the data was recorded at
questions.explanation: Eigenvectors of a covariance matrix are its principal directions; the corresponding eigenvalues are the variances along those directions — the eigenvector with the largest eigenvalue is the long axis of the sample cloud's shape.

questions.text: If a covariance matrix's larger eigenvalue is much bigger than its smaller one, the sample cloud (plotting x(n) against x(n-1)) looks like:
choices: A thin, elongated ellipse along the principal eigenvector's direction
A perfect circle with no preferred direction
questions.explanation: A large ratio between the eigenvalues means most of the variability is concentrated along one direction (the principal eigenvector), producing a thin, elongated ellipse rather than a round, isotropic cloud.

questions.text: The Karhunen-Loeve expansion represents a random process as:
choices: A sum of its covariance matrix's eigenvectors, each weighted by an uncorrelated random coefficient
A single deterministic formula with no randomness
The Fourier transform of its mean function
questions.explanation: Karhunen-Loeve is the formal bridge between random processes and eigendecomposition: the process is rebuilt from its covariance matrix's own eigenbasis, with uncorrelated random weights on each eigenvector.

questions.text: Why do structured, correlated signals tend to concentrate energy into a few large covariance-matrix eigenvalues, while unstructured noise doesn't?
choices: Structure means strong correlation along a few directions; unstructured noise spreads its variance roughly evenly across every direction
Noise always has a larger total variance than any real signal
Eigenvalues are undefined for noisy data
questions.explanation: A structured signal's samples are strongly correlated along a low-dimensional subspace, pushing most of the covariance matrix's energy into a handful of large eigenvalues. Unstructured noise has no such preferred direction, so its energy spreads roughly evenly across all eigenvalues — the basis of subspace-based detection.

questions.text: A 2x2 covariance matrix has diagonal entries both equal to 6 and one eigenvalue equal to 9. Using the fact that eigenvalues sum to the matrix's trace, what is the other eigenvalue?
questions.explanation: Trace = 6 + 6 = 12. Eigenvalues sum to the trace, so the second eigenvalue is 12 − 9 = 3.

questions.text: A 2x2 covariance matrix has diagonal entries both equal to 4 and off-diagonal entries equal to 0 (uncorrelated equal-variance samples). What are both eigenvalues equal to?
questions.explanation: With zero off-diagonal entries the matrix is already diagonal, so its eigenvalues are just the diagonal entries themselves: both equal to 4. Zero correlation means no preferred direction — the sample cloud is a circle, not an ellipse.

questions.text: One phrase: eigenvalue-ranked detectors that separate a few large eigenvalues (signal) from a noise floor of small, roughly equal eigenvalues are called ___-based detectors.
questions.explanation: Subspace-based detectors exploit exactly this eigenvalue separation — a low-dimensional signal subspace of large eigenvalues sitting above a noise floor of small, roughly equal ones.

questions.text: Cumulative review: for the eigendecomposition ideas in this module to apply, the covariance matrix must be symmetric and positive semi-definite. Which property from the linear-algebra module guarantees a symmetric matrix's eigenvalues are real (not complex)?
choices: The spectral theorem for symmetric (Hermitian, for real matrices) matrices
The fact that all 2x2 matrices have real eigenvalues
The requirement that the matrix be invertible
questions.explanation: From linear-algebra-for-signals: the spectral theorem guarantees that any real symmetric (or complex Hermitian) matrix has real eigenvalues and an orthogonal (unitary) eigenbasis — exactly the property that makes 'variance along a direction' a meaningful, real-valued idea for covariance matrices.
